title: Don't Get Scrapped: Essential Things to Never Skip on a Cheap Car
metaDescription: Buying a budget car? Don't get caught out! Discover the essential things to never skip on a cheap car to find a reliable, honest deal. Drive smart with BuyAndScrap.com!
targetKeyword: things to never skip on a cheap car
content:
When buying a cheap car, you must never skip thorough pre-purchase checks, a detailed physical inspection, a comprehensive test drive, and ideally, a professional mechanic's assessment. These essential **things to never skip on a cheap car** ensure you spot hidden issues, avoid costly repairs, and secure a reliable vehicle. At BuyAndScrap.com, we help you find honest, affordable cars, but knowing what to look for is your best defence.

## Why Thorough Checks Matter on a Budget Car

Buying an affordable car is smart. However, a low price can hide big problems. Skipping vital checks now often leads to expensive repairs later. Our experience shows that a little vigilance saves a lot of hassle.

Investing time in these checks protects your wallet and provides peace of mind. Every step helps you make an informed decision.

## Before You See the Car: Essential Things to Never Skip

Before you even view a vehicle, crucial detective work is needed. This pre-screening is one of the most important **things to never skip on a cheap car** hunt. It helps you rule out risky options early.

### MOT History Check

Always check the car's MOT history on the Gov.uk website. Enter the registration number to see its full record. Look for recurring advisories, failed tests, or significant gaps in testing.

This history reveals how well the car has been maintained. Our free [MOT Checker](/mot-checker) can help you quickly access this information.

### Comprehensive Vehicle History (HPI) Check

Never skip a full HPI check. For a small fee, it confirms if the car has outstanding finance, has been written off, stolen, or has an inaccurate mileage reading. This check is non-negotiable for your safety and investment.

It provides crucial details that sellers might not disclose. Protecting yourself from hidden issues is key.

### Research Common Faults

Spend a few minutes researching common problems for your chosen make and model online. A quick search can reveal recurring issues or recalls. Knowing these helps you inspect the car more effectively.

This foresight allows you to specifically look out for known weaknesses. It's like having inside information before you buy.

## The Physical Inspection: What to Look For

Once you're face-to-face with the car, it's time for a thorough visual inspection. Think like a detective looking for clues.

### Exterior: Rust and Panel Gaps

Check for rust around wheel arches, sills, the boot lid, and windows. Surface rust isn't ideal, but deep, bubbling rust signals serious trouble. Peek underneath for rust on suspension components and the exhaust.

Also, check panel gaps. Are the spaces between the bonnet, doors, and boot even? Uneven gaps can indicate poor quality accident repairs.

### Tyres: Tread, Condition, Consistency

Inspect all four tyres, plus the spare if accessible. Check the tread depth; it must be above the legal minimum of 1.6mm across 75% of the tyre's width. Look for cracks, bulges, or uneven wear.

Uneven wear often points to suspension or alignment problems. Ensure all tyres are from similar brands; mismatched tyres can sometimes indicate budget-cutting.

### Under the Bonnet: Fluids, Belts, Hoses

Ask the seller to open the bonnet. Check engine oil, coolant, and brake fluid levels. Look for strange colours, such as milky oil or rusty coolant, which signal serious issues. Also, check for any leaks under the car.

Inspect drive belts and hoses for cracks or fraying. These components are expensive to replace if they fail unexpectedly.

### Interior Checks: Wear and Electrics

Examine the interior for excessive wear on seats, steering wheel, and pedals. This can reveal high mileage or heavy use. Test all electricals: lights, windows, air conditioning, radio, and dashboard warning lights.

Ensure all warning lights illuminate then switch off after starting. Any persistent warning lights mean a problem. Also, check for damp carpets or a musty smell, which could indicate water leaks.

## The Crucial Test Drive

Never buy a car without a proper test drive. This is your chance to feel how the car performs on the road. Drive on various roads and speeds.

Always ask the seller to accompany you. Take your time during the drive.

### Starting Up and Idle

Listen carefully when you start the engine. Does it turn over easily? Is there any smoke? Once running, listen for unusual noises like knocking or rattling at idle. The engine should sound smooth and steady.

### Brakes, Steering, Suspension

Test the brakes; they should feel firm and stop the car smoothly without pulling to one side. Check the steering for looseness or excessive play. Drive over speed bumps or uneven surfaces to test the suspension; listen for clunks or squeaks.

### Gearbox and Clutch

With a manual car, check the clutch. Does it bite too high? Is it stiff? Smoothly shift through all gears. With an automatic, ensure gear changes are seamless and not jerky. Listen for grinding noises.

## Professional Opinion: Don't Skip a Mechanic's Check

Even after your own thorough checks, consider having an independent mechanic inspect the car. This is perhaps the most important of all **things to never skip on a cheap car** purchase. A professional can spot issues you might miss.

They have specialised tools and expertise to identify potential failures. This small investment can save you thousands in future repairs.

## FAQs: Buying a Cheap Car

### Is it safe to buy a cheap car?

Yes, it can be safe to buy a cheap car, provided you conduct all necessary inspections and checks. A low price doesn't automatically mean a bad car, but diligence is crucial to find a reliable one.

### How much should I spend on an HPI check?

An HPI check typically costs between £15-£25. This small fee is a vital investment to protect against serious hidden issues like outstanding finance or a history of being written off.

### What's the legal tyre tread depth in the UK?

The legal minimum tyre tread depth in the UK is 1.6mm across the central three-quarters of the tread width, around the entire circumference of the tyre.

## Ready to Find Your Next Car?

Finding a great car on a budget is absolutely possible with the right approach. By following these essential checks, you empower yourself to make a smart, confident decision. For more advice, explore our [guides](/guides).

Ready to browse honest, affordable cars? Start your search today. We're here to help you drive away happy.
